title: Off-chain session token from verified handshake
description: After a successful on-chain handshake verification, emit a signed JWT (or SD-JWT) session token that encodes the verified scope, nullifiers, and expiry. Downstream tool calls can present this token instead of re-proving on-chain, enabling sub-100ms auth for agentic workflows. The token references the on-chain transaction hash as its trust anchor. This is the missing bridge between ZKP correctness and real-time agentic commerce latency requirements — without it, every tool call requires a fresh proof or an on-chain transaction, making Bolyra unusable for high-frequency agent-to-agent interactions.

title: Payment-gated scope escalation via escrow binding
description: Add a new public input to AgentPolicy that binds a proof to an on-chain escrow contract address and deposit amount. The verifier checks that the escrow is funded before accepting FINANCIAL_MEDIUM or FINANCIAL_UNLIMITED scope bits. This closes the gap between 'agent is authorized to spend' and 'agent has provably locked funds for the operation' — critical for agentic commerce where agents autonomously transact. Implementation: add escrowBinding = Poseidon2(escrowAddress, depositAmount) as a public output, verify the escrow contract state in IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake().

title: Agent credential rotation without re-enrollment
description: Implement a rotateAgentCredential flow that lets an operator update permissionBitmask or expiryTimestamp for an existing agent without removing and re-inserting the Merkle leaf. The operator signs a rotation token linking old and new credential commitments; the contract updates the tree in-place and emits a CredentialRotated event. Without rotation, any permission change requires tree removal + re-enrollment, invalidating all in-flight delegation chains and breaking long-running agent sessions — a dealbreaker for production agentic commerce.

title: Hosted testnet playground with one-click handshake
description: Deploy a public Base Sepolia instance of IdentityRegistry with pre-enrolled test identities, a web UI that walks through enrollment → handshake → delegation in 3 clicks, and a curl-friendly REST API wrapping the SDK. Include pre-built WASM proving bundles so the browser generates real proofs without Node. This directly attacks the adoption score (17-19 range) — external developers currently need to compile circuits, download ptau files, and run Hardhat locally before seeing a single proof verified. The playground should be the landing page CTA.

title: Verifiable receipt chain for multi-agent pipelines
description: Define a receipt format where each agent in a pipeline signs a receipt linking its handshake proof, the previous agent's receipt hash, and the tool-call output hash. The chain of receipts is independently verifiable without replaying proofs. Store receipt Merkle roots on-chain at intervals (batched). This is the 10-year play: agentic commerce needs auditable, non-repudiable transaction trails. The receipt chain turns Bolyra from 'auth at the gate' into 'provenance throughout the pipeline.' Implementation: new ReceiptChain.sol contract + SDK receipt builder (~200 lines each).

title: Define application/bolyra-proof+cbor media type and proof envelope schema
description: The protocol lacks a standardized wire format for transmitting proofs between parties. Define a CBOR-based proof envelope with a registered media type (application/bolyra-proof+cbor) containing fields: proof_system (groth16|plonk), circuit_id, public_signals[], proof_bytes, metadata{}. Write an ABNF grammar in spec/ and register the media type per RFC 6838. This enables interop between independent SDK implementations and third-party verifiers without reverse-engineering the JS SDK's internal serialization.

title: Complete DID resolution algorithm with on-chain state lookups
description: spec/did-method-bolyra.md defines the DID method but the resolution algorithm is underspecified — it doesn't describe how a resolver maps did:bolyra:<commitment> to a DID Document by querying IdentityRegistry state (humanTree membership, agentTree membership, revocation status). Implement a concrete resolution algorithm section with pseudocode, define the DID Document structure including verificationMethod entries for the EdDSA keys, and add a service endpoint for the handshake protocol. This is required for W3C DID Core conformance.

title: Machine-readable conformance test vectors for all three circuits
description: Create spec/test-vectors/ with JSON files covering: valid handshake (human+agent), expired agent credential, stale Merkle root, scope subset violation, cumulative bit encoding violation, delegation chain (1 and 3 hops), nonce replay, and revoked identity. Each vector includes named inputs, expected witness outputs or expected failure reason. A conformance runner (spec/conformance-runner.js) loads vectors and runs them against the circuit witness generator. This is the minimum bar for any alternative implementation to prove compatibility.

title: Add human tree root history buffer matching agent tree's 30-root buffer
description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but humanTree has no such buffer — any new enrollment immediately invalidates in-flight human proofs. This is an interop hazard: a compliant verifier cannot know whether a human proof was generated against a recently-valid root. Add the same ROOT_HISTORY_SIZE=30 ring buffer for humanTree roots, with an isKnownHumanRoot() view function. Update the IETF draft's Security Considerations to document the root staleness window.

title: Add blinding salt to scope commitment to prevent brute-force
description: scopeCommitment = Poseidon2(bitmask, credCommitment) is deterministic over a 256-value bitmask space (8 bits). An observer can precompute all 256 possible commitments for a known credCommitment and determine the exact permission set. Change to Poseidon3(bitmask, credCommitment, blindingSalt) where blindingSalt is a private random field element. Update AgentPolicy, Delegation circuits, SDK proof generation, and the IETF draft's privacy analysis section. This is a protocol-level privacy fix that must happen before any production deployment.

title: Replace chained Poseidon2 calls with single Poseidon(N) in Delegation circuit
description: The Delegation circuit computes delegatorCredCommitment by accepting it as a private input and then re-derives scopeCommitment via separate Poseidon calls. By restructuring to use a single Poseidon(4) for the delegation token and combining the two scope commitment hashes (delegator verification + delegatee output) into fewer hash rounds, we can save ~600 constraints (~2% of total). More importantly, this eliminates the risk of hash-input ordering mismatches between circuit and SDK that have caused silent verification failures in similar projects.

title: Evaluate replacing EdDSA-Poseidon with ECDSA-secp256k1 via efficient Groth16 gadgets for operator signatures
description: EdDSA-Poseidon verification costs ~6,000 constraints per signature. ModelInstanceBinding has two EdDSA verifiers (~12,000 constraints). For operator signatures specifically, using circom-ecdsa with precomputed tables could align with existing Ethereum key infrastructure (operators already have secp256k1 keys), eliminating the need for a separate EdDSA keypair. This is a feasibility study + prototype: measure constraint count of the efficient circom-ecdsa gadget vs current EdDSAPoseidonVerifier, and determine if the adoption benefit (reuse existing keys) outweighs any constraint increase.

title: Add variable-depth short-circuit to BinaryMerkleRoot to reduce average proving time
description: All three circuits use BinaryMerkleRoot(20) but the tree is often sparse — early enrollments sit at depth 1-5. The circuit already accepts merkleProofLength but the underlying template still evaluates all 20 levels via Mux1 gates. Adding an explicit depth-check constraint that zeros out unused sibling hashes and skips the Poseidon round for padded levels would reduce average witness computation time by 30-50% for trees with <1000 leaves. This directly improves proving latency in the critical handshake path.

title: Move timestamp freshness check out of AgentPolicy circuit into on-chain verifier
description: AgentPolicy includes a LessThan(64) comparator for currentTimestamp < expiryTimestamp inside the circuit (~128 constraints + the Num2Bits(64) range check on currentTimestamp). Since currentTimestamp is already a public input verified on-chain (IdentityRegistry checks block.timestamp proximity via AgentTimestampStale), the in-circuit comparison is redundant — the contract can do `require(currentTimestamp < expiryTimestamp)` for free. Removing the in-circuit LessThan saves ~256 constraints and eliminates a subtle soundness issue: if currentTimestamp is attacker-chosen (public input), the in-circuit check provides no additional security beyond what the contract enforces.

title: Ship WASM witness generator alongside C++ for browser-side proving
description: Currently the SDK relies on rapidsnark (native binary) for production proving. For browser and serverless environments, there is no fallback — snarkjs full-JS proving is 10-50x slower. Compile each circuit's witness generator to WASM via circom --wasm (already supported by circom 2.1.6), bundle the .wasm files in the SDK, and add a prove() path that uses snarkjs.groth16.fullProve with the WASM witness calculator. This gives sub-3-second browser proving for HumanUniqueness (~15k constraints) without requiring native binaries, unlocking client-side use cases.

title: Cross-chain Merkle root sync via EIP-1186 storage proofs
description: Deploy IdentityRegistry on Arbitrum and Polygon alongside the existing Base Sepolia deployment. Implement a CrossChainRootRelay contract that accepts EIP-1186 storage proofs of humanTree and agentTree roots from the canonical chain (Base) and updates a mirrored rootHistory buffer on secondary chains. This avoids bridge trust assumptions — any relayer can submit the proof, and the target chain verifies the MPT inclusion against the L1 state root exposed by each rollup's precompile (ArbSys.arbBlockHash on Arbitrum, the L1 state oracle on Polygon zkEVM). Estimated 3 new contracts: CrossChainRootRelay.sol, IRootOracle.sol interface, and per-chain oracle adapters.

title: Bind sessionNonce to chainId to prevent cross-chain proof replay
description: The current nullifierHash and sessionNonce are chain-agnostic — a valid handshake proof generated for Base can be replayed on Arbitrum if both chains share the same Merkle roots (which they will after root sync). Fix: change nullifier derivation in all three circuits to include a chainId public input: nullifierHash = Poseidon3(scope, secret, chainId) for HumanUniqueness, and similarly for AgentPolicy and Delegation. Update IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake to pass block.chainid as the expected chainId public signal. This is a breaking change to the circuit layout — requires regenerating .zkey and verifier contracts.

title: Cross-chain nonce deduplication via shared nullifier set
description: Even with chainId binding, the usedNonces mapping is per-chain. An operator could use the same sessionNonce on two chains for two distinct handshakes, doubling the effective session count. Implement a NullifierBroadcast contract that, after each successful verifyHandshake, emits a cross-chain message (via Hyperlane or LayerZero) containing the nullifierHash. Receiving chains store it in usedNullifiers before their own verification accepts it. Include a configurable grace period (e.g., 10 minutes) where the nullifier is 'pending' to account for cross-chain latency. Add a view function isNullifierGloballyUsed(uint256) that checks both local and relayed sets.

title: Chain-local agent enrollment with federated root aggregation
description: Currently all agent enrollments go through a single IdentityRegistry. For multi-chain operation, operators on Arbitrum shouldn't need to transact on Base just to enroll an agent. Implement chain-local agentTree instances where each chain maintains its own LeanIMT for agent credentials enrolled there. A FederatedRootAggregator contract on the canonical chain periodically collects per-chain agentTree roots (via storage proofs) and computes a super-root = Poseidon(baseRoot, arbRoot, polygonRoot). The AgentPolicy circuit's Merkle proof then proves membership against any sub-tree, with the super-root as the on-chain public input. Requires a new BinaryMerkleRoot wrapper that first selects the sub-tree index.

title: Add human tree root history buffer matching agent tree's 30-root buffer
description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but none for humanTree — a new human enrollment immediately invalidates all in-flight human proofs. This is especially painful in multi-chain scenarios where cross-chain root relay adds 5-15 minutes of latency. Add a matching uint256[30] humanRootHistory buffer and isValidHumanRoot(uint256) check, updated on each enrollHuman call. Update verifyHandshake to validate the human proof's Merkle root against this buffer instead of requiring an exact match to the current root. This is a targeted fix to the existing IdentityRegistry.sol with no circuit changes required.

title: Add domain separation tags to all nullifier hash computations
description: HumanUniqueness computes nullifier as Poseidon2(scope, secret) and AgentPolicy computes it as Poseidon2(secret, sessionNonce). Both use Poseidon2 with no domain separator, meaning a collision is possible if an attacker can arrange scope == secret and secret == sessionNonce across the two circuits. Prepend a unique domain tag constant (e.g., hash of circuit name) as the first Poseidon input in each circuit: Poseidon3(HUMAN_DOMAIN, scope, secret), Poseidon3(AGENT_DOMAIN, secret, sessionNonce), Poseidon3(DELEG_DOMAIN, ...). This is a standard formal-verification-driven hardening — Semaphore v4 itself uses scope for domain separation but Bolyra's cross-circuit nullifier space is wider. Update the verifier contracts and SDK to match the new public signal layout.

title: Prove merkleProofLength bounds and enforce depth range check in circuit
description: All three circuits accept merkleProofLength as a private input passed to BinaryMerkleRoot but never constrain it to be > 0 or <= MAX_DEPTH within the circuit itself. BinaryMerkleRoot silently uses depth 0 if merkleProofLength is 0, producing root == leaf, which means any commitment trivially 'proves' membership. Add explicit range constraints: assert 1 <= merkleProofLength <= MAX_DEPTH via LessThan comparators. Write a formal property spec (in FORMAL-PROPERTIES.md) proving that no valid witness exists with depth outside [1, MAX_DEPTH].

title: HumanUniqueness lacks any temporal liveness constraint
description: AgentPolicy and Delegation both enforce currentTimestamp < expiryTimestamp, and IdentityRegistry checks timestamp staleness for agent/delegation proofs. But HumanUniqueness has no expiry or timestamp input at all — a human proof is valid forever once generated, even after the identity is revoked (revocation only blocks future enrollments, not existing proofs with valid Merkle roots still in history). Add an optional expiryEpoch public input to HumanUniqueness with a liveness check, or document the formal invariant that human proof liveness is solely enforced by root rotation in the contract (and write the property proving this is sufficient given the root history buffer size).

title: Write and mechanically check scope monotonicity invariant across delegation chains
description: The Delegation circuit enforces delegateeScope & ~delegatorScope == 0 (subset) per hop, and IdentityRegistry chains hops via lastScopeCommitment. But no formal property currently proves that the composition of N hops preserves monotonicity end-to-end — specifically that hop N's effective scope is a subset of hop 0's original scope. Write a formal induction proof (as a standalone Circom test template or a property in FORMAL-PROPERTIES.md) that: (1) the subset relation is transitive under the bitwise encoding, (2) the cumulative-bit invariant (bit 4 → bits 2+3) is preserved by the subset operation, and (3) the on-chain chaining via scopeCommitment hashes prevents an adversary from injecting a wider scope mid-chain.

title: Adversarial test vectors for field-overflow and wrap-around attacks
description: The circuits use Num2Bits(64) range checks on permissionBitmask, expiryTimestamp, and currentTimestamp. However, there are no test vectors verifying that inputs >= 2^64 (or close to the BN254 scalar field order r) are correctly rejected by the witness generator. Build a test harness with at least 20 adversarial vectors: field-order-minus-1 for each ranged input, values at 2^64, 2^64+1, r-1, r-permissionBitmask (wrap-around to valid-looking value), and zero. Verify each produces a witness generation failure. This catches any underconstraint that Num2Bits might miss due to field arithmetic edge cases.

title: HumanUniqueness nullifier lacks nonce domain separation
description: In HumanUniqueness.circom, nullifierHash = Poseidon2(scope, secret). The sessionNonce is bound separately via nonceBinding but does NOT feed into the nullifier. This means a human who proves once for a given scope can never prove again for that scope — even in a different session with a different nonce. If 'scope' is a static constant like hash('bolyra-handshake-v1'), the human gets exactly ONE handshake ever. Either the nullifier must include sessionNonce (making it per-session), or the contract must not store human nullifiers permanently — but IdentityRegistry uses usedNonces which is per-nonce, not per-nullifier. Clarify the design intent and add an explicit test proving a human CAN complete two handshakes with different nonces under the same scope, or fix the nullifier derivation.

title: currentTimestamp is a private input with no on-chain freshness bound in AgentPolicy
description: AgentPolicy takes currentTimestamp as a public input and checks expiryTimestamp > currentTimestamp inside the circuit. However, the prover chooses currentTimestamp. The contract checks for staleness via a tolerance window (DelegationTimestampStale / AgentTimestampStale errors), but the tolerance window size is critical — if it's too wide (e.g., 1 hour), an attacker with an expired credential can backdate currentTimestamp to just before expiry and still pass the circuit check, then submit the proof within the tolerance window. Audit the exact tolerance in IdentityRegistry, tighten to ≤60 seconds, and add a test where an expired-by-1-minute credential with a backdated timestamp is rejected.

title: Delegation circuit does not verify delegator's Merkle inclusion
description: The Delegation circuit verifies the delegatee's Merkle inclusion (CIP-1 fix for phantom delegatee) but takes delegatorCredCommitment as a bare private input with no Merkle proof that the delegator is enrolled in agentTree. An attacker can fabricate a delegatorCredCommitment that hashes to match the previousScopeCommitment (from a legitimate AgentPolicy proof) but corresponds to no enrolled agent. While exploiting this requires a Poseidon preimage attack (computationally infeasible), defense-in-depth demands the delegator also prove tree membership. Add a second BinaryMerkleRoot check for delegatorCredCommitment, or document the explicit security assumption that Poseidon preimage resistance is the sole binding.

title: scopeCommitment is brute-forceable (8-bit bitmask, public credCommitment)
description: In AgentPolicy, scopeCommitment = Poseidon2(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment). The permissionBitmask is only 8 meaningful bits (256 values). If credentialCommitment is known or guessable (it's the Merkle leaf, observable on-chain from enrollment events), an observer can brute-force all 256 Poseidon2 hashes in <1ms and learn the agent's exact permissions from the public scopeCommitment. This breaks the privacy model described in Delegation.circom ('scope bits are PRIVATE'). Fix: add a random blinding salt as a third Poseidon input — scopeCommitment = Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, blindingSalt) — making brute-force infeasible.

title: humanTree has no root history buffer — proofs go stale on any enrollment
description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree (preventing valid proofs from failing due to tree updates during proof generation), but humanTree has no equivalent buffer. The contract verifies humanMerkleRoot against the current humanTree root only. If any new human enrolls between when a prover generates their proof and when they submit it on-chain, the root changes and the proof is rejected. With Groth16 proving taking 5-30 seconds and enrollment being permissionless, this is a practical griefing vector: an attacker can front-run handshake transactions with enrollments to invalidate proofs. Add a matching 30-root history buffer for humanTree.

title: Typed error hierarchy with machine-readable codes for SDK and contracts
description: The current SDK throws generic `new Error(string)` and the IdentityRegistry uses unparameterized custom errors (e.g., `StaleAgentRoot()` with no context). Replace SDK errors with a typed `BolyraError` class hierarchy carrying `code` (enum like `STALE_ROOT`, `NONCE_MISMATCH`, `SCOPE_SUBSET_VIOLATION`), `context` object, and human-readable `message`. On the Solidity side, add indexed parameters to custom errors (e.g., `StaleAgentRoot(uint256 providedRoot, uint256 latestRoot)`). Publish the error code enum in both `@bolyra/sdk` and `bolyra` Python package so integrators can programmatically catch and recover from specific failure modes without string-parsing.

title: Fluent delegation chain builder with compile-time scope narrowing
description: The existing delegation API requires manually constructing Merkle proofs, scope bitmasks, and chaining `previousScopeCommitment` across hops — a footgun-rich surface. Build a `DelegationChain.from(agentCredential).narrowScope(Permission.FINANCIAL_SMALL | Permission.READ_DATA).delegateTo(delegateeCredential).build()` fluent builder that validates cumulative bit encoding at construction time (TypeScript generics prevent expanding scope), auto-fetches Merkle proofs from the registry, and serializes the chain into the wire format the contract expects. The builder produces a `DelegationChainProof` that `verifyHandshake` already knows how to consume.

title: Self-describing proof envelope with content-type and version negotiation
description: Currently proofs are bare JSON arrays of bigints with implicit positional semantics — integrators must know that `publicSignals[0]` is `humanMerkleRoot` and `publicSignals[4]` is `nonceBinding`. Define a `BolyraEnvelope` codec (msgpack or CBOR with JSON fallback) that wraps proofs with `{ version: '0.2', circuit: 'HumanUniqueness', provingSystem: 'groth16', publicSignals: { humanMerkleRoot, nullifierHash, nonceBinding, ... }, proof: {...} }`. Add `BolyraEnvelope.encode()` / `BolyraEnvelope.decode()` to both TS and Python SDKs. This eliminates the `publicSignals[5]` positional indexing that caused the chainId mismatch bug in the cross-chain experiment.

title: Zero-config quickstart: 5 lines from npm install to verified handshake
description: The current `proveHandshake` requires the caller to resolve WASM/zkey paths, construct Merkle proof objects, and manage nonce generation. Add a `BolyraClient` class with `new BolyraClient({ provider })` that auto-discovers circuit artifacts (embedded via `postinstall` or resolved from `@bolyra/artifacts`), fetches Merkle proofs from the on-chain registry, generates session nonces, and exposes `client.handshake(humanSecret, agentCredential)` returning a verified result. Target: `npm install @bolyra/sdk && const result = await new BolyraClient({provider}).handshake(secret, cred)` — 5 lines including import.

title: Type-safe permission bitmask helpers with cumulative encoding validation
description: Developers currently hand-construct 64-bit permission bitmasks as raw numbers and must manually ensure cumulative bit invariants (bit 4 implies 2+3). Ship `Permission` enum, `Permissions.from(Permission.FINANCIAL_MEDIUM, Permission.READ_DATA)` builder that auto-sets implied bits, `Permissions.includes(mask, Permission.WRITE_DATA)` checker, and `Permissions.narrow(parent, child)` that throws `ScopeExpansionError` at construction time. Expose in both TS (`@bolyra/sdk`) and Python (`bolyra.permissions`). This prevents the most common integration bug: forgetting cumulative bit implications and getting silent circuit failures.

title: LangChain CallbackHandler for automatic handshake on tool invocation
description: Build a `BolyraAuthCallbackHandler` that hooks into LangChain's `on_tool_start` lifecycle. Before any tool tagged as `requires_auth=True` executes, the handler automatically runs `proveHandshake()` via the Python SDK subprocess bridge, attaches the proof to the tool's metadata, and short-circuits with a descriptive error if verification fails. This eliminates the need for developers to manually weave auth into every chain — they just add the callback handler to `invoke()`. Ship as `bolyra-langchain` on PyPI with a `<10 lines to auth` quickstart.

title: CrewAI task delegation → Bolyra delegation circuit mapping
description: CrewAI's `Task.delegate()` passes work between agents but has no cryptographic scope enforcement — any delegatee can act with the delegator's full permissions. Build a `BolyraDelegationTool` that intercepts CrewAI task delegation, maps the task's required capabilities to Bolyra's 8-bit permission bitmask, generates a Delegation proof with scope narrowing, and injects the `newScopeCommitment` into the delegatee's context. The delegatee must present this proof before executing privileged operations. This makes CrewAI multi-agent workflows auditable with on-chain delegation chains.

title: Off-chain session tokens with batched on-chain checkpoints
description: Framework users running multi-step agent chains (e.g., a 20-tool LangChain sequence) cannot afford an on-chain `verifyHandshake` per step. Add a `BolyraSession` class that performs one on-chain handshake at session start, then issues short-lived off-chain session tokens (HMAC over nullifierHash + sessionNonce + expiry). Tools verify the token locally in <1ms. A background task batches all session token hashes into a Merkle tree and posts the root on-chain at configurable intervals (default: every 60s or 50 operations). This gives framework users 100x throughput with periodic on-chain anchoring.

title: AutoGen GroupChat ZK auth middleware
description: AutoGen's `GroupChat` allows multiple agents to converse freely, but there's no mechanism to verify agent identity or restrict which agents can participate. Build a `BolyraGroupChatManager` subclass that requires each agent to present a valid `AgentPolicy` proof (with appropriate `requiredScopeMask`) before joining the chat. The manager verifies proofs against the on-chain registry and maintains a per-session nullifier set to prevent agent impersonation. Expose the verified permission bitmask so the `speaker_selection_method` can factor in agent capabilities.

title: Framework-friendly structured error codes with recovery hints
description: The current contract reverts with generic errors like `InvalidAgentProof()` and `StaleAgentRoot()`, which are opaque to framework developers building retry logic. Replace each error with a structured `BolyraError` enum carrying a machine-readable code (e.g., `AGENT_ROOT_STALE_30`), a human message, and a `recoveryHint` field (e.g., 'Re-fetch agentTree root; your proof was generated against a root that has rotated out of the 30-slot history buffer'). Expose these in both the TS and Python SDKs so LangChain/CrewAI error handlers can automatically retry with fresh proofs instead of failing the entire chain.
